title: Anger at the Therapist Who Was a Cult Leader
dek: *When the person who was supposed to help you untangle your pain instead wove you into their own web of control, the anger that rises is not pathology but proof of life. This is about what happens when the guide becomes the cage, and how you find your way back to trusting your own inner knowing when trust itself has been weaponized against you.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Anger at a therapist who abused power through cultish dynamics is a healthy signal that your boundaries were violated. The body holds this betrayal as trauma, and naming what happened begins the reclamation of your own authority. Healing requires distinguishing between the legitimate therapeutic relationship you sought and the manipulation you received.

Have you been carrying a quiet fury toward someone who was supposed to witness your healing, only to realize years later that what you experienced wasn't therapy at all? The question sits differently in the body than garden-variety disappointment with a counselor. This is the particular rage that comes when you discover the framework itself was rigged. When the person holding space for your vulnerability was simultaneously constructing an architecture of dependence. When "unconditional positive regard" was actually grooming. In my own practice, I've sat with people who spent years believing their anger at a former therapist was resistance to growth, only to later recognize they were responding to coercion dressed in therapeutic language. The body knew before the mind caught up. ## The Specific Shape of Therapeutic Betrayal Cult dynamics in therapy don't always look like robes and chanting. More often, they appear as subtle boundary erosions. The therapist who begins sessions on time but lets them run long when you're crying, training you to perform distress for attention. The practitioner who shares just enough personal struggle to make you feel chosen, special, entrusted with their humanity in a way that inverts the professional container. Robert Augustus Masters writes about spiritual bypassing as the use of spiritual ideas to sidestep psychological work, and therapeutic cultism operates in the same register. The language of healing becomes the vehicle for harm. "You're not ready to leave yet" gets framed as clinical assessment rather than control. "Your resistance to this practice shows how much you need it" becomes unfalsifiable. The anger you feel is your psyche's immune response to this invasion. It's the part of you that never fully bought the story, even when you were trying hardest to believe it. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to anger as information. When a therapist operates like a cult leader, your anger is reading the room correctly. It's registering the gap between the stated intention (your healing) and the actual dynamic (your subordination). The rage is proportional not to imperfect care but to the exploitation of the very vulnerability you offered in good faith. ## When Healing Language Becomes Control Language The specific vocabulary matters. Cult-like therapists often borrow from legitimate modalities, mixing genuine technique with manipulation so thoroughly you can't tell where one ends and the other begins. They might use real somatic practices while simultaneously fostering dependence on their unique interpretation. They reference valid trauma research while positioning themselves as the sole translator of your experience. You might notice this in retrospect: the way every insight you had was reframed as their teaching. The way your progress was always incomplete, requiring another intensive, another private session, another level of commitment. The way leaving was pathologized as abandonment of yourself rather than honored as autonomy. Soraya Chemaly's work on women's anger examines how we're socialized to doubt our rage, to interpret it as personal failing rather than social reality. When that therapist suggests your anger at them is projection, transference, or unresolved attachment trauma, they're using this exact conditioning. They're counting on you to turn the anger inward, to assume the problem is your perception rather than their behavior. But your body doesn't lie about power. The tightness in your chest when you remember certain sessions, the way your shoulders rise when you imagine confronting them, the nausea that surfaces when you think about how much money you paid, how much time you gave, these are not symptoms to be managed. They're data. ## The Physiology of Realizing You Were Harmed There's a particular somatic signature to this recognition. It often arrives not as a single revelation but as a slow-building awareness, like developing a photograph in a darkroom. Details that seemed insignificant at the time, the way they'd touch your shoulder, the jokes about other clients, the comments about your appearance, suddenly reorganize into a pattern. When the pattern clicks into focus, the body responds. Some people describe it as heat rising from the solar plexus, a volcanic pressure that's been dormant. Others feel it as a sudden cold clarity, ice water through the veins. The anger might arrive with shaking, with tears that feel different from sadness, with an urge to physically move, to push something away. This is your nervous system finally permitted to have the response it was suppressed from having in real time. In the therapy room, you were in a one-down position, dependent on this person's approval, working hard to be a good client, a receptive student, an open vessel. The anger couldn't fully land then because it would have threatened your access to what you believed was help. Now, outside that container, your body can tell the truth. The anger is late, but it's right on time. ## Distinguishing Therapeutic Rupture from Cultic Harm Not every bad therapy experience is a cult. Not every therapist who makes mistakes is a predator. The distinction matters because it shapes your healing path. Ordinary therapeutic rupture happens when a clinician misspeaks, misreads a situation, or brings their own unresolved material into the room in ways that cause harm. A good therapist can acknowledge this, repair the relationship, or support you in ending treatment if that's what you need. The power differential exists, but it's held consciously, with ethical boundaries and external accountability. Cultic harm is structurally different. It involves: Isolation from other perspectives (discouraging you from talking to other therapists, friends who question the work, or family members who express concern). Escalating commitment (financial, temporal, emotional investments that increase over time rather than leading toward independence). Thought-stopping techniques (mantras, practices, or reframes that shut down critical thinking). Elitism (the suggestion that this approach, this therapist, this community has access to truth others don't). Exploitation (sexual, financial, labor, or emotional extraction that benefits the therapist). When you're inside it, these elements feel like deep work, like finally being understood, like the rigor required for real transformation. From outside, they look like confinement. Your anger helps you see the shape of the cage you've stepped out of. In our experience, people often oscillate between rage and doubt, wondering if they're overreacting, if it was really that bad, if maybe they just weren't ready for the work. This oscillation itself is often a residue of the dynamic, the way you were trained to question your own knowing. ## What Your Anger Knows That You're Still Learning The fury you feel carries information you haven't fully unpacked yet. It knows you were lied to, even if you can't articulate every lie. It knows you were used, even if the use was subtle. It knows the relationship was fundamentally unequal in ways that exceeded the necessary asymmetry of the therapeutic frame. Thomas Moore writes about the soul's need for its own darkness, its own descent, without someone else's agenda imposed on the journey. A cult-like therapist colonizes your darkness. They tell you what your dreams mean, what your body is saying, what your resistance reveals. They position themselves as essential to your access to your own depths. Your anger is reclaiming that territory. It's saying: my psyche is mine. My pain is mine to be with. My healing happens at my pace, not yours. This doesn't mean you do it alone. It means you get to choose your companions, to work with practitioners who understand that their job is to make themselves obsolete, not indispensable. It means you have permission to be discerning, even suspicious, of anyone who suggests they have special access to your truth. The anger also knows something about time. It knows how much you lost, how many years you spent in a dynamic that wasn't serving you. This might be the sharpest edge of the rage, the grief-soaked fury at your own younger self for not seeing it sooner, for not leaving earlier. But you were doing the best you could with the information you had. The therapist held the power and the expertise. They were trained to create and maintain these dynamics. You were seeking help, which requires a degree of openness that can be exploited. The anger at yourself can soften when you remember this asymmetry. ## A Five-Minute Practice for When the Rage Arrives When the anger surges and you need to be with it without being consumed by it, try this: Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Feel the ground under you. Take a breath deep enough that your belly moves. Now let yourself make fists with your hands, tight enough that you feel it in your forearms. Hold for a slow count of five. Release. Repeat three times. As you do this, you might say internally or aloud: "This anger is information. This anger is protection. This anger is mine." Let the words be simple, rhythmic, something your body can hold onto. If tears come, let them. If you need to move, walk, or shake out your arms, do that. The goal isn't to discharge the anger completely but to be present with it, to let it move through you rather than storing it as tension. When you're ready, place one hand on your heart and one on your belly. Breathe into the space between them. You're here now. You got out. The anger helped you leave, and it will help you rebuild. ## Rebuilding Trust in Your Own Discernment One of the cruelest aftereffects of therapeutic cultism is the erosion of your ability to trust your own judgment. If you were manipulated by someone whose job was to help you think more clearly about your life, how can you trust yourself to choose wisely going forward? This is where the anger becomes fuel. It sharpens your boundaries. It makes you less willing to override your gut when something feels off. It teaches you that politeness is not worth your safety, that staying in a harmful dynamic to avoid disappointing someone is a recipe for further harm. You might find yourself drawn to [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) as you work with the physiological dimensions of this anger. [Bach Holly Flower Remedy (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005VW2I7K?tag=spankyspinola-20) has been used traditionally for feelings of hatred and envy, but in my own practice I've seen it support people working with the clean, clarifying anger that comes from boundary violations. It doesn't suppress the feeling but helps you stay present with it. Some people benefit from working with omega-3 fatty acids during this process. [Nordic Naturals Ultimate Omega (120 softgels)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B002CQU54Q?tag=spankyspinola-20) supports nervous system function, and the body needs that foundation when it's processing betrayal trauma. This isn't about numbing out but about giving your system the resources it needs to metabolize difficult emotional material. You might also explore [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) we've assembled, which offers practices for working with the heat of anger without letting it burn you or go cold. The goal is to stay warm, alive, responsive. ## When to Seek Support and What to Look For You don't have to do this alone, but choosing your next support carefully matters enormously. Here's what to look for if you decide to work with another therapist: Someone who practices informed consent, explaining their approach and your options clearly. Someone who has clear boundaries around time, money, and contact outside sessions. Someone who has external supervision and accountability. Someone who doesn't position themselves as having special powers or unique access to truth. Someone who explicitly works toward your independence, not your dependence. Red flags include: pressure to commit to long-term contracts upfront, discouragement from seeking second opinions, suggestion that you're too fragile for the outside world, sexual or romantic boundary crossings (even subtle ones), financial exploitation, or any replication of the dynamics you're trying to heal from. [The assessments hub](/assessments) we maintain includes questions to ask a potential therapist before you begin working together. You have permission to interview them, to take your time, to walk away if anything feels off. You might also benefit from peer support, from others who've experienced therapeutic abuse. There are online communities and local groups where people share strategies and validation. You're not alone in this, even though the experience can be profoundly isolating. Over the years I've seen people move through this with the support of friends, bodyworkers, spiritual communities that don't demand allegiance, and sometimes another therapist who understands the specific contours of this kind of harm. The path isn't linear, but it is possible. ## The Anger That Becomes Discernment As you work with this rage over time, something shifts. The heat that initially felt like it might consume you begins to clarify into something more refined. You start to notice manipulation in real time rather than months later. You trust your no. You don't need to justify or explain your boundaries to yourself or others. This is the anger doing its proper work. It's not a permanent state but a phase of reclamation, a burning away of the stories you were told about yourself by someone who had no right to define you. The research is clear on this: according to the CDC's guidance on stress and the body, unprocessed emotional experiences contribute to chronic stress and its physiological consequences. Your anger needs somewhere to go, some way to be witnessed and metabolized, or it will keep showing up as tension, as sleeplessness, as the background hum of unsafety. You might keep [RESCUE Bach Original Flower Remedies, Remedy®, Natural Stress Relief, 0.7 fl oz](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01N9BEUCV?tag=spankyspinola-20) nearby for moments when the intensity spikes unexpectedly. These moments will become less frequent, but they deserve gentle attention when they arrive. The community at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) has been exploring these territories of anger and reclamation, and you might find resonance in those conversations. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### How do I know if what I experienced was actually therapeutic abuse or if I'm just bitter about therapy not working? The distinction often lives in your body's response and in the structural elements of the relationship. If you find yourself unable to make decisions without imagining what that therapist would say, if you were financially exploited, if there were sexual or romantic boundary crossings, if you were isolated from other relationships, if your autonomy decreased rather than increased over time, these are indicators of abuse rather than simply ineffective treatment. Ineffective therapy might feel frustrating or disappointing. Abusive therapy feels like a violation, a betrayal, a theft of your agency. Trust that distinction. ### Is it normal to still feel angry years after leaving a harmful therapeutic relationship? Yes. Betrayal trauma, especially when it involves someone in a position of care and authority, can take years to fully process. The anger might arrive in waves, triggered by anniversaries, by seeing something that reminds you of that time, by reaching new levels of understanding about what happened. This isn't a sign that you're stuck. It's often a sign that you're finally safe enough to feel what you couldn't feel when you were still in the dynamic. Let the anger have its seasons. ### Should I confront my former therapist or report them to a licensing board? This is a deeply personal decision with no single right answer. Some people find confrontation or reporting to be part of their healing, a way to speak truth and potentially protect others. Others find that it retraumatizes them or that the systems aren't set up to handle these complaints effectively. Consider what you need for your own healing first. If reporting feels important, gather documentation and perhaps consult with a therapist who specializes in professional ethics violations. If confrontation feels important, consider doing it in writing or with support present. And if neither feels right, that's valid too. Your healing doesn't require their acknowledgment. ## The Authority You're Reclaiming What you're doing now, this work of naming what happened and feeling the appropriate rage about it, is an act of profound self-loyalty. You're choosing your own reality over the reality someone else constructed for you. You're trusting your body's wisdom over someone else's interpretation of your experience. This is how you become your own authority. Not by never needing support or guidance, but by knowing the difference between collaboration and colonization. By recognizing when someone is walking beside you and when they're trying to own the path. The anger will shift and change. Some days it will feel like fire, some days like ice, some days like the quiet certainty that you deserved better and you're building better now. All of these are part of the process. The through-line is your growing capacity to trust yourself, to honor what you know, to say no to what doesn't serve you and yes to what does. You're not broken for being angry. You're awake. The work now is to let that awakeness inform everything you build from here, every relationship you choose, every boundary you hold, every time you listen to the voice inside that knows what's true for you. That voice was always there. The anger is just helping you hear it again. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on anger at the therapist who was a cult leader, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
